topic: Design Refresh Phase A — Item 1: masthead stance statement (proposal review)
proposal_review_focus: Per Step 4 of curmudgeon.md and the curmudgeon_review_note in proposal-EXP-208-draft-ready.json, this review attacks the proposal package itself — not non-existent prose. Focus areas: (1) above- vs below-h1 placement; (2) italic justification on dark; (3) max-width:56ch editorial cadence; (4) verbatim preservation of four load-bearing moves; (5) hero-thesis-deferral correctness. Plus generic adversarial review: dependency claims, fallback behaviour, test-assertion robustness, scope creep, NC-violations.
our_argument_summary: EXP-208 inserts an italic serif eyebrow paragraph (.stance-statement) above the existing 'Critical Review' h1 inside <div class="title-block">, consuming EXP-202's var(--serif) and var(--ink-2) tokens, with four test.js assertions locking verbatim text, DOM order, serif role, and ink-2 colour. Hero thesis sentence is explicitly deferred to a future EXP. Status DRAFT_AWAITING_HUMAN_GROUP_REVIEW with a hard hold on integration until operator posts HNOTE-DESIGN-REFRESH-PHASE-A-INTEGRATION-APPROVED-NNN naming EXP-208.

verbatim_text_check.method: Diffed the stance text in EXP-208 change_1 (verbatim_text and to_substring) against HNOTE-DESIGN-REFRESH-PHASE-A-001 SCOPE Item 1 'DRAFT STANCE STATEMENT'.
verbatim_text_check.hnote_source: The world is a globe. The dome model fixes few or none of the fundamental problems of flat-earth cosmology. That does not prevent us from engaging with the specific claims it does make — and that is what this document does.
verbatim_text_check.exp_text: The world is a globe. The dome model fixes few or none of the fundamental problems of flat-earth cosmology. That does not prevent us from engaging with the specific claims it does make — and that is what this document does.
verbatim_text_check.diff: byte-identical including the U+2014 em dash and the trailing period
verbatim_text_check.finding: All four load-bearing moves are preserved verbatim. CC7 honoured. No analyst cadence drift introduced.

proposal_question_answers.Q1_above_vs_below_h1: Defensible. The brief literally says 'above the hero thesis sentence in the masthead.' The hero thesis isn't in the masthead yet, so the brief's instruction is forward-looking. The analyst interprets this as 'above the current first h1 (Critical Review)'. The interpretation is reasonable AND forward-compatible: when the hero thesis lands as a child of .title-block (analyst's stated plan, between the Ovoid Cavity h1 and the .subtitle paragraphs), the stance remains above it. ALTERNATIVE the human group might prefer: place the stance between the two h1s (so 'Critical Review' stays the first thing readers see, with the stance subtitling it). The EXP's anticipated_objection #3 surfaces this trade-off and the from/to substrings make the swap mechanical. No curmudgeon objection.
proposal_question_answers.Q2_italic_justification: Mostly defensible, with one residual risk the analyst already names. Italic serif at 0.95rem on dark CAN read soft on low-DPI Windows even with -webkit-font-smoothing:antialiased. The EXP flags this in notes_for_human_review_group ('DARK-THEME ITALIC RENDERING WINDOWS NON-RETINA') and offers a font-weight:450 step-up if integration testing exposes the issue. That is appropriate handling. The ironic/undermining read is real but mitigated by (a) the eyebrow weight, (b) the immediately concessive second sentence, (c) the editorial-deck convention. No objection from the curmudgeon — flag forwarded to human group as the EXP intends.
proposal_question_answers.Q3_max_width_56ch: Defensible. 56ch is the editorial-deck convention (Pentagram, NYT Magazine, Commarts cited). Anticipated_objection #5 addresses the visual-mismatch concern reasonably. The narrowness IS the design signal that this is a preamble, not a second subtitle. If the human group prefers full-width, the swap is a one-line CSS edit. No objection.
proposal_question_answers.Q4_verbatim_preservation: Confirmed verbatim — see verbatim_text_check above. CC7 honoured. test.js assertion #1 is the tamper-evidence enforcement. No objection.
proposal_question_answers.Q5_hero_thesis_deferral: Defensible. The brief Item 1 explicitly says 'The hero thesis sentence (to be agreed with the human review group in a subsequent EXP, candidate below) remains the visual hero.' The analyst reads 'in a subsequent EXP' as licence to defer the hero thesis to a follow-on EXP. CC5 addresses this. Counter-reading worth noting: the brief could also be read as 'land both in this EXP, where the hero thesis is the candidate the human group then approves/revises.' Both readings are defensible; the analyst chose the more conservative one (defer), which is consistent with the HARD HOLD discipline and reduces blast radius if the human group wants to revise. No objection.

holes_found.description: FALSE FALLBACK CLAIM: ordering_constraint_note (EXP-208 line 5) and notes_for_human_review_group 'DEPENDENCY ON EXP-202' both assert that if EXP-202 is held when EXP-208 lands, 'the stance statement still renders correctly via the serif fallback chain (Georgia/Times New Roman/serif) but without the Source Serif 4 optical tuning' and 'the fallbacks (Georgia serif, hardcoded colour) still work.' This is factually incorrect. The proposed CSS uses bare var() with NO fallback argument: 'font-family:var(--serif)' and 'color:var(--ink-2)'. Per CSS Custom Properties spec, when a referenced custom property is undefined, var() triggers Invalid At Computed-Value Time (IACVT); for inherited properties (font-family, color) the property then resolves to the inherited value, NOT to a fallback chain. Existing :root in generate-html.js (line 430) does NOT define --serif or --ink-2 today — those tokens come from EXP-202, which is also held (status: DRAFT_AWAITING_HUMAN_GROUP_REVIEW). So if EXP-208 is approved and integrated while EXP-202 is still held, .stance-statement will inherit body's font-family ('Segoe UI',Arial,Helvetica,sans-serif) and body's color (var(--text) — #222 light / #e0e0e0 dark). The italic serif preamble becomes an italic SANS-SERIF preamble in body-coloured text — losing both the editorial register and the secondary-ink differentiation. The four test.js assertions still pass (they check that the rule and tokens are present in source, not that they resolve at computed-value time), so the bug ships silently. Worth noting: the stance statement does NOT 'break' in the sense of the page failing to render — it just renders in the wrong font and the wrong colour, which is precisely the load-bearing visual character of the change.
holes_found.recommendation: Choose one: (a) PREFERRED — change the two var() declarations to use proper fallback syntax, e.g. 'font-family:var(--serif, Georgia, "Times New Roman", serif)' and 'color:var(--ink-2, #b8b5ad)' (with a separate light-mode rule for the light-theme #3A352D); this honours the 'no strict dependency' framing; (b) ALTERNATE — promote EXP-202 from 'recommended ordering' to a strict dependency and require the operator approval HNOTE for EXP-208 to also approve EXP-202 (or to come AFTER EXP-202's approval HNOTE has landed and integrated); (c) WORST — keep the current var() declarations but correct the EXP narrative to say 'EXP-208 must not be integrated before EXP-202; the fallback claim is wrong'. The analyst should pick (a) or (b) and re-issue the draft.

holes_found.description: TEST ASSERTIONS DO NOT CATCH THE FALLBACK FAILURE: assertion #3 ('font-family:var(--serif)') and assertion #4 ('color:var(--ink-2)') are source-text checks against the generated HTML's <style> block. They confirm the CSS rule is present with the right tokens, but they CANNOT confirm the tokens resolve at computed-value time — that requires a headless-browser computed-style check, not a regex match. So if EXP-208 ships without EXP-202, the four assertions all pass while the rendering silently breaks. This is partially redundant with the major finding above (fixing var() fallback syntax mostly resolves the rendering issue) but flagged separately because the assertions LOOK comprehensive and may give the human review group false confidence.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) accept that the 'recommended ordering' (EXP-202 first) is the operating contract and rely on integration sequencing to prevent the misconfigured state, OR (b) add a fifth assertion that uses Playwright (already a project dep for PDF generation) to load docs/index.html in a headless browser and assert getComputedStyle('.stance-statement').fontFamily contains 'serif' (case-insensitive) and getComputedStyle('.stance-statement').color is one of the two expected RGB values. Heavier but catches resolution failures. Given the EXP is already classified FOUNDATIONAL_MODEL_CHANGE and is held until operator approval, the lighter (a) path is acceptable IF EXP-202-first ordering is enforced.

holes_found.description:
DOM-ORDER ASSERTION FRAGILITY: assertion #2 uses regex /
[\s\S]*?<\/div>/ to extract the title-block, then checks /stance-statement[\s\S]*?

after
. Today the title-block contains no nested
, so this works. But if any future EXP introduces a nested div inside the title-block (e.g., a flex container around h1+stance, or a wrapper for the hero thesis sentence when it lands), the regex will truncate at the first inner
and may miss the h1 entirely — producing a false-FAIL or, worse, a false-PASS depending on what falls inside the truncated slice. The current EXP is fine, but the assertion is brittle to follow-on EXPs in this same masthead area (and Phase A has explicit follow-on EXPs A4, A5, A6, A7 that may touch the masthead).
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) use a balanced HTML parser (cheerio / linkedom — neither is currently a dep, so this is a small new dep), OR (b) use a less-fragile regex that matches the title-block's specific closing pattern (e.g., capturing up to the next <div class="scorecard"... opening, which is the structural sibling), OR (c) accept the brittleness and add a comment in test.js noting that future EXPs touching .title-block must verify this assertion still works. Lowest-cost is (c) — which is also documentation, not a code change.

holes_found.description: HERO-THESIS COORDINATION GAP: the EXP defers the hero thesis sentence to a subsequent EXP and notes (in cross_references) that the analyst envisions the hero thesis going 'between the Ovoid Cavity h1 and the .subtitle paragraphs'. But the brief's language ('the hero thesis sentence... remains the visual hero') is somewhat ambiguous about whether the hero thesis REPLACES the existing 'Critical Review' h1, lives alongside it, or is positioned elsewhere. The EXP-208 placement (stance ABOVE 'Critical Review') is forward-compatible with the analyst's stated envisioned hero-thesis placement (between Ovoid Cavity h1 and .subtitle), but is NOT forward-compatible with a placement where the hero thesis becomes the FIRST element in the masthead — in that scenario the stance would need to move up again. The EXP doesn't surface this coordination question for the human review group.
holes_found.recommendation: Add to notes_for_human_review_group a sub-bullet under 'HERO THESIS SENTENCE IS NOT IN THIS EXP' explicitly listing the three placement options for the future hero thesis (option A: between Ovoid Cavity h1 and .subtitle — analyst default; option B: replace 'Critical Review' h1; option C: above the stance statement, becoming first), and noting that option C would require revisiting EXP-208's placement. Cheap to add; prevents an ambiguity from compounding.

holes_found.description: PRINT @media OVERRIDE INHERITS THE SAME var() ISSUE: the EXP claims 'No additional print rule is needed' because var(--ink-2) in print resolves to #3A352D via EXP-202's print override. But if EXP-202 is held when EXP-208 ships, the print --ink-2 is also undefined and color falls through to body's var(--text) = #222. Print rendering becomes 'italic body-font dark grey on white' — which is closer to the editorial intent than the dark-mode failure mode but still loses the secondary-ink differentiation. Same root cause as the major finding; resolves automatically when var() fallbacks are added.
holes_found.recommendation: No separate fix — folds into the major finding's recommendation.

holes_found.description: VERBATIM REGEX CONTAINS A LITERAL EM DASH: assertion #1 has the U+2014 em dash inline in the regex. Node.js reads source as UTF-8 by default, so this works in normal CI. But two edge cases bear flagging: (a) if test.js is ever served through a build step that ASCII-escapes non-ASCII source, the em dash regex would fail to match the rendered HTML's em dash; (b) if a future contributor copy-pastes the regex via a tool that normalises punctuation (some IDEs auto-correct dashes), the em dash could silently degrade to a hyphen and the assertion would fail. Both are unlikely but worth a one-line comment in test.js noting that the dash is intentionally U+2014.
holes_found.recommendation: Either (a) add a code comment // U+2014 em dash — do not normalise above the assertion, OR (b) rewrite the literal em dash as the unicode escape \u2014 in the regex string (slightly less readable, more robust). Either is fine; comment is cheaper.

stronger_arguments.description: PROACTIVELY RESOLVE THE var() FALLBACK QUESTION FOR ALL Phase A EXPs that consume EXP-202 tokens. EXP-208 will not be the only EXP with this dependency — EXP-207 (typography application), EXP-A4 (verdict bar chart), EXP-A5 (palette restriction), EXP-A6 (accuracy demotion), EXP-A7 (Latest Findings demotion), EXP-A8 (a11y) will all likely consume --serif, --sans, --ink, --ink-2, --rule, --semantic-* tokens. The cleanest resolution is a project-wide rule: any var() consuming an EXP-202 token MUST use fallback syntax until EXP-202 is integrated, then the fallbacks become redundant safety nets. Worth raising at the design-refresh-phase-a EXP-202 review level, not just here.
sources: MDN: CSS custom property fallback semantics

stronger_arguments.description: CONSIDER AN INTEGRATION-ORDER GATE IN THE APPROVAL HNOTE SCHEMA. The brief's HNOTE-DESIGN-REFRESH-PHASE-A-INTEGRATION-APPROVED-NNN currently has approved_exp_ids: [...]. Add an optional ordered_after: [...] field so an approval HNOTE can express 'approve EXP-208 only after EXP-202 is in main'. This puts the ordering constraint in the approval mechanism itself, removing reliance on any individual analyst correctly noting the dependency. Small schema add, large robustness gain across Phase A.

kernel_of_truth.description: The proposal package is unusually thorough. Every CC1–CC7 is explicitly addressed, every NC1–NC7 has a stated preservation mechanism, all seven anticipated objections have substantive rebuttals (not boilerplate), the four load-bearing moves are preserved verbatim, scope is correctly held to the masthead with no out-of-scope drift, the hero-thesis deferral is well-justified, the test-assertion design correctly trades coverage for tamper-evidence, the print/mobile/dark theme considerations are addressed, and the integration-hold is correctly enforced with a named approval HNOTE pattern. The single factual error (var() fallback claim) is the kind of error a careful reviewer catches — but the surrounding work is strong enough that a fix is mechanical, not structural.
kernel_of_truth.why_it_doesnt_save_claim: Thoroughness doesn't excuse the var() fallback error. The 'no strict dependency' framing is a load-bearing claim about when the EXP is safe to integrate; if it's wrong, the human review group could approve EXP-208 in isolation under the false assumption that fallbacks protect them. The fix is simple, but the fix must be made before integration, and the EXP narrative must be corrected so the human group makes the integration decision against accurate facts. Without the fix, this is a draft that should not advance to operator approval as-is.

recommended_action_detail: Two narrow edits: (1) change 'font-family:var(--serif)' to 'font-family:var(--serif, Georgia, "Times New Roman", serif)' and add an explicit hardcoded colour fallback strategy for var(--ink-2) (either var() with fallback in the rule, plus a separate light/dark @media wrapper, OR promote EXP-202 to a strict blocker and remove the 'no strict dependency' framing); (2) update ordering_constraint_note and notes_for_human_review_group entry 'DEPENDENCY ON EXP-202' to accurately describe what happens if EXP-202 is held. Optionally also add the hero-thesis-coordination-gap clarification (moderate finding 4) and the assertion-regex em-dash comment (minor finding 6). Keep verbatim text, four load-bearing moves, eyebrow weight, italic styling, 56ch max-width, and the four test.js assertions as-is. Re-issue the draft as a c2 cycle for human review group consumption.

citations_verified: HNOTE-DESIGN-REFRESH-PHASE-A-001 source text — diffed against monitor/analyst/human-notes.json, verbatim match including U+2014 em dash
EXP-202 existence and DRAFT status — confirmed at monitor/analyst/expansions/EXP-202.json
title-block structure in build-scripts/generate-html.js (line 1236) — confirmed two h1s (Critical Review, Ovoid Cavity), no nested div
current :root tokens (line 430) — confirmed --serif and --ink-2 NOT defined; only EXP-202 supplies them
citations_failed: ordering_constraint_note's 'serif fallback chain (Georgia/Times New Roman/serif)' claim — fails verification: bare var(--serif) without fallback argument does not invoke the chain when --serif is undefined; falls through to inheritance (Segoe UI sans-serif from body)

consider_other_side: A defender of the EXP as-drafted could argue: (1) the 'recommended ordering' is documented and the decider's integration logic should respect it, so the var() fallback question is academic — answer: 'recommended ordering' is not enforced by any pipeline check today; the EXP narrative explicitly says 'no strict dependency'; the human review group could approve EXP-208 in isolation and the integrator would have no programmatic gate to catch it. (2) Even if the rendering degrades, the page doesn't break, so the cost is cosmetic — answer: this EXP is FOUNDATIONAL_MODEL_CHANGE on the masthead; degraded cosmetic rendering on the masthead is precisely the failure mode the entire Phase A design refresh is intended to prevent ('the design asks the reader to take the author's word for it'). (3) The fix is so small it can be made at integration-time without re-issuing the draft — answer: no, because the EXP narrative IS what the human review group reviews; if the narrative is wrong, the review is uncalibrated. Re-issue is cheap. The strongest defender position is to accept the moderate findings and request the minor_edit pass; the major finding has no good defence.
notes_to_decider: This proposal review is for the curmudgeon-review-requested gate per proposal-EXP-208-draft-ready.json. Do NOT integrate EXP-208 — integration is hard-held until operator posts HNOTE-DESIGN-REFRESH-PHASE-A-INTEGRATION-APPROVED-NNN naming EXP-208. This review provides adversarial input for the human review group's consideration; the analyst should treat the major + 3 moderate + 2 minor findings as the punch list for a re-issued c2 draft. After re-issue, push a new priority-queue item for curmudgeon to confirm the var() fallback fix is correct.
